narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away feels like a door slamming shut just as you're reaching for the handle. Our prediction model looked at your profile and saw an 85% chance of struggling to pass the bar exam down the line, painting a picture of a path fraught with hurdles based on patterns from thousands of students who've walked it before. What stood out most was your LSAT score of 37, the strongest signal pulling toward this tough outlook, as it suggests the kind of analytical firepower needed for bar success might be a steep climb from here. Layered on top was your Year 1 decile of 2, showing early law school grades that placed you low in your class, which the model saw as the second-biggest red flag for building momentum. Your Year 3 decile of 1 reinforced that worry even further, hinting at a consistent challenge in keeping pace with the demands of legal studies. Even with your high family income offering some stability, it unexpectedly tipped the scales toward failure in the model's eyes, perhaps reflecting patterns where such backgrounds don't always translate to the grit needed under pressure. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 added to the caution, as did subtler factors like being a white female pursuing full-time studies—none were deal-breakers alone, but together they formed a story of risk the model couldn't ignore. This isn't about your potential or heart; it's the model connecting these dots to protect you from investing in a program where the odds feel stacked. Many in similar spots have turned things around by targeting those early weak spots—like boosting test prep or grade habits—before reapplying stronger. You've got time to rewrite this chapter; what small step feels right for you next?
